agent1_snippets.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Function
agent1_snippets.content: Monoclonal antibodies against COVID-19 disrupt host cell entry by binding the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (AE2) receptor binding domain (RBD) of the virus. SARS-CoV-2 engages the host cell ACE2 receptor via the spike (S) glycoprotein to cause infection. This makes spike S glycoprotein on the coronavirus surface a common target for antiviral therapy, most notable of which are monoclonal antibodies. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Etesevimab -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Many glycosylated S proteins layer the surface of SARS-CoV-2, helping the virus bind to the host cell's ACE2 (Angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 receptor) and facilitating viral entry. The S protein has 2 subunits: S1 and S2. The S1 subunit comprises NTD (N-terminal domain) and RBD (receptor-binding domain). [7] The RBD of the S1 subunit binds to the host cell's ACE2. The RBD is a primary target for producing neutralizing monoclonal antibodies. Etesevimab, bamlanivimab, casirivimab, and imdevimab are neutralizing monoclonal antibodies available against SARS-COV-2. Etesevimab and bamlanivimab bind to the overlapping epitopes in RBD (see Figures. Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab, Bamlanivimab, Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab). Casirivimab and imdevimab bind to non-overlapping epitopes of RBD. [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Function -- Mechanism of Action of Monoclonal Antibodies Against SARS-CoV-2
agent1_snippets.content: The pathophysiology of COVID-19 is described by the entry of SARS-CoV-2, the causative virus, into the hosts' cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors expressed abundantly on the respiratory epithelium, such as type II alveolar epithelial cells. [9] This process is mediated by the receptor-binding domain (RBD) on the spike protein followed by priming of the spike protein (S2) by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [10] Monoclonal antibodies prevent the viral attachment by binding to a non-overlapping epitope on the surface spike protein RBD of SARS-CoV-2 with high affinity, thereby blocking the binding of the virus to the human ACE2 receptor.

agent1_snippets.title: Bamlanivimab -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: SARS-CoV-2 is a single-stranded enveloped RNA virus. The outer surface comprises the envelope, membrane, and many glycosylated S-proteins. The virus binds to the host cell's angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) receptor via S-protein. The S-protein comprises 2 subunits, S1 and S2. With the help of the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of the S1 subunit, the virus binds to the ACE-2 receptor found in cells of the respiratory system, gastrointestinal system, and endothelium. [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Bamlanivimab -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Bamlanivimab is a neutralizing immunoglobulin G1 variant mAb to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. It comprises 2 identical light and heavy chain polypeptides composed of 214 and 455 amino acids, respectively. This antibody blocks S-protein attachment to human ACE-2 receptors, preventing subsequent viral entry and replication (see Image. Bamlanivimab Mechanism of Action). The drug binds an epitope on RBD in both open and closed confrontation, which led this mAb to be potentially used as a monotherapy in phase 2 of the Blocking Viral Attachment and Cell Entry with SARS-CoV-2 Neutralizing Antibodies (BLAZE-1) trial. [8] Bamlanivimab also showed antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity on reporter cells following engagement with target cells that express spike protein in the in-vitro study. This study also revealed enhanced viral clearance via an intrinsic immune response and the mAb infusion. However, with emerging variants and resistance, the FDA revoked the EUA for monotherapy in April 2021. The EUA for combination therapy of bamlanivimab and etesevimab was reinstated in August 2021 with the caveat of unauthorizing the use of the combination therapy where the frequency of resistance to the variants exceeds 5%.

agent1_snippets.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction -- Pathogenesis of COVID-19
agent1_snippets.content: The pathogenesis of COVID-19 is a complex interplay between direct tissue injury from viral invasion and a heightened immune response from the host. The early phase of the disease is characterized by viral entry into the host cells. The SARS-CoV-2 virus enters the host cells by attaching to the ACE-2 receptors that are present in abundance in the respiratory epithelium, the nasal mucosa, esophagus, etc. [1] . This attachment is specifically between the spike protein S1 and the ACE-2 receptors. After the initial attachment, the viral spike protein subunit S2 primes the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) and facilitates the process of endocytosis, or cell entry. Once the virus has gained entry into the cell, it replicates and assembles virions [2] . See Figure. Endocytosis of SARS-CoV-2.

[10] This receptor is found on the respiratory epithelium, upper esophagus, ileum, myocardium, proximal tubular cells in the kidney, and the urothelium of the bladder. agent1_snippets.title: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: The SARS coronavirus (SARS-CoV) uses the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor for entry into its host. [4] SARS-CoV recognizes the host ACE2 receptor through the virus's receptor-binding domain. Mutation in this domain may allow this and other coronaviruses to cause new and increased cross-species infections. [11] The virus concentrates in the lungs and small bowel, which are areas with a high density of its receptors. [4] Specifically, the alveolar epithelium shows the highest focus of infection. [4] The infection leads to serous pleural effusions, pulmonary edema, and consolidations with relative sparing of the upper respiratory tract. [4] The lung injury is thought to be due to an out-of-control immune response by the host leading to excess quantities of pro-inflammatory cytokines. [4] [12] Some patients will develop bacterial (i.e., Staphylococcus aureus, Strenotrophomonas maltophilia ), viral, or fungal (i.e., Aspergillus , Candida ) superinfections. [4] The histopathology features, which have been found during postmortem and may not be representative of all SARS cases, show epithelial cell proliferation, diffuse alveolar damage, and macrophage infiltration of the lung. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Mechanical Ventilation and Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Considerations in COVID-19 -- Anatomy and Physiology
agent1_snippets.content: The SARS-CoV-2 virus is a single-stranded RNA virus belonging to the Betacoronavirus genus and consists of four major structural proteins identified in the pathogenesis of COVID-19 disease. [1] The spike protein (S) is considered the main structural protein that protrudes from the viral surface and gives it a “corona-like" appearance. The S protein binds to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE 2) receptors of the human cells leading to endocytosis of the virus. Other proteins include the membrane (M) protein, envelope (E) protein, and nucleocapsid (N) protein that plays a significant role in infectivity and viral replication. [2] The ACE 2 receptors are expressed highly in the nasal, pharyngeal, and bronchial mucosa and are the primary source of entry for the virus. [3] Other mucosal surfaces such as the gastrointestinal tract, kidneys, and conjunctiva also express ACE 2 receptors and have been found to be affected by the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

agent1_snippets.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: The engineered EVs also carry great potential as a therapy for several diseases; this includes cancer, immune-associated diseases, neurological, metabolic, and infectious diseases. Scientists adopted several strategies to engineer EVs for therapeutic purposes. One approach is to insert proteins on the membrane of the vesicle that has a therapeutic benefit or that target EVs to a specific type of cells like cancer cells. They also modified the content of the EVs to carry a particular protein or nucleic acid that would combat a particular disease. Grafting of the EVs with artificial nanoparticles is another strategy that could improve the kinetics or the targeting of the EVs. Many potential anticancer and antimicrobial therapies have been invented and at different stages of the trials. For example, EVs carrying ACE2, the receptor of the COVID19 virus, showed promising results regarding their use as antiviral medicine. [38]

agent1_snippets.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: All five reported VOCs -Alpha(B.1.1.7); Beta(B.1.351); Gamma (P.1); Delta(B.1.617.2); and Omicron (B.1.1.529) have mutations in the RBD and the NTD, of which N501Y mutation located on the RBD is common to all variants except the Delta variant which results in increased affinity of the spike protein to ACE 2 receptors enhancing the viral attachment and its subsequent entry into the host cells. Along with NBD, RBD serves as the dominant neutralization target and facilitates antibody production in response to antisera or vaccines. [4] Two recent preprints reported that a single mutation of N501Y alone increases the affinity between RBD and ACE2 approximately ten times more than the ancestral strain (N501-RBD). Interestingly the binding affinity of the Beta (B.1.351) variant and Gamma (P.1) variant with mutations N417/K848/Y501-RBD and ACE2 was much lower than that of N501Y-RBD and ACE2. [5] [6] The mutations seen in Omicron are described below.

agent1_snippets.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Genetic variation in the viral genes of SARS-CoV-2 can have implications for its pathogenesis, especially if it involves the RBD, which mediates viral entry into the host cells and is an essential target of vaccine sera monoclonal antibodies. As described previously, the three reported VOCs (B.1.1.7; B.1.351; and P.1) have mutations in the RBD and the NTD, of which N501Y mutation located on the RBD is common to all variants and results in increased affinity of the spike protein to ACE 2 receptors enhancing the viral attachment and its subsequent entry into the host cells. Although the respiratory system is the predominant target for SARS-CoV-2, as described above, it can affect other major organ systems such as the gastrointestinal tract (GI), hepatobiliary, cardiovascular, renal, and central nervous systems. SARS-CoV-2–induced organ dysfunction, in general, is possibly explained by either one or a combination of the proposed mechanisms, such as direct viral toxicity, ischemic injury caused by vasculitis, thrombosis, or thrombo-inflammation, immune dysregulation, and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) dysregulation. [43]

agent1_snippets.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Function -- Antiviral Activity of Baricitinib
agent1_snippets.content: The potential antiviral activity of Baricitinib is embedded in the mechanism of viral cell entry of several viruses including SARS-CoV-2. The virus gains entry into the cells by a process known as clathrin-mediated endocytosis [12] . Clathrin is a protein that is found in abundance on the inner surface of the cell membrane, this protein is involved in making the vesicle-like covering around the viral particle leading to endocytosis/entry into the cell. The first step in the process involves the binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the host cell surface receptor / ACE 2. This leads to the activation of 2 kinases, the AP2-associated protein kinase 1 (AAK-1) as well as the cyclin D-associated kinase (GAK). These two kinases namely the AAK-1 and the GAK lead to the activation of host proteins called the adaptor protein complexes (APs). These APs recruit clathrin to form the cage-like structure around the virus leading to endocytosis. Baricitinib has been shown to inhibit AAK-1, and this has been the basis of the proposed antiviral action [13] . This mechanism of action was suggested by a London-based machine learning study (Benevolent) that evaluated a large repository of approved medications and found potential inhibitory activity at clinically achievable serum concentrations. Baricitinib uniquely showed a high affinity for AAK-1 in comparison to other JAK inhibitors like Tofacitinib and Upadacitinib [14] . It should be noted that SARS-CoV-2 utilizes several endocytotic pathways for viral entry and this benefit could easily be circumvented with viral utilization of an alternate endocytotic pathway [15] . Image 2 depicts the proposed antiviral action of Baricitinib.

agent1_snippets.title: Ophthalmic Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: The pathogenesis and tissue tropism of SARS-CoV-2 are linked to the interaction between the viral spike protein and its cognate receptor on human host cells— the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) receptor. Efficient cell entry requires cleavage by the protein transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2). ACE-2 expression is predominant in respiratory mucosal and alveolar epithelial cells but has also been identified in other tissues, including the gastrointestinal tract, kidney, vascular endothelial cells, immune cells, and even neurons. Virulence is achieved through direct cellular invasion and death and the induction of widespread cytokine-driven inflammation and vascular leakage. [15] Additionally, immune cell and complement debris may contribute to an increased thromboembolic state.

agent1_snippets.title: Features, Evaluation, and Treatment of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology -- Effect of SARS-CoV-2 on the Respiratory System
agent1_snippets.content: Increased vascular permeability and subsequent development of pulmonary edema in patients with severe COVID-19 are explained by multiple mechanisms. [51] [52] [53] These mechanisms include: Endotheliitis as a result of direct viral injury and perivascular inflammation leading to microvascular and microthrombi deposition Dysregulation of RAAS due to increased binding of the virus to the ACE2 receptors Activation of the kallikrein-bradykinin pathway, the activation of which enhances vascular permeability Enhanced epithelial cell contraction causes swelling of cells and disturbance of intercellular junctions The binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the Toll-Like Receptor (TLR) induces the release of pro-IL-1β, which mediates lung inflammation until fibrosis . [54]

agent1_snippets.title: Casirivimab -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Casirivimab is a recombinant neutralizing human immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal antibody to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. It binds to the S1 subunit of the spike protein receptor-binding domain (RBD), blocking the attachment of SARS-CoV-2 to the human ACE2 receptor. This prevents viral binding to the host cell, thereby preventing entry and replication of the virus, thus decreasing the viral load. Imdevimab binds to a non-overlapping portion of the spike protein RBD, similar to casirivimab, and the combination is theorized to limit the development of viral mutations. The cocktail of the 2 drugs was also found to have retained activity against the Alpha (B.1.1.7; UK), Beta (B.1.351; South Africa), Gamma (P.1; Brazil), Delta (B.1.617.2; India), Epsilon (B.1.427/429; California), Iota (B.1.526; New York), and Kappa (B.1.617.1; India) variants of SARS-CoV-2. The combination drug has no activity to the Omicron variant. [5] [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.content: The surface spike (S) glycoprotein, which resembles a crown, is located on the outer surface of the virion. It undergoes cleavage into an amino (N)-terminal S1 subunit, which facilitates the incorporation of the virus into the host cell. The carboxyl (C)-terminal S2 subunit contains a fusion peptide, a transmembrane domain, and a cytoplasmic domain responsible for virus-cell membrane fusion. [43] [44] The S1 subunit is further divided into a receptor-binding domain (RBD) and an N-terminal domain (NTD), which facilitates viral entry into the host cell and serves as a potential target for neutralization in response to antisera or vaccines . agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2532
agent2_snippets.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers are frequently employed together as a combination therapy, representing a specific class of antihypertensive regimens that integrate two distinct mechanisms of action to achieve more effective blood pressure control. By pairing the renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system inhibition of ARBs with the vasodilatory effects of calcium channel blockers, clinicians can enhance therapeutic efficacy, reduce the likelihood of dose‑related side effects, and provide a versatile option for patients who require multiple agents to reach target blood pressure goals. This combined approach is recognized as a standard strategy in the management of hypertension and related cardiovascular conditions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1145
agent2_snippets.content: 1H‑tetrazole is a chemical scaffold that is present in both cilostazol and aliskiren, linking these two drugs through a common structural motif. The entities amidy and azoly are also related to cilostazol as substructures, and azoly is similarly connected to aliskiren, while amidy is mapped to aliskiren, indicating a close chemical relationship between amidy and the renin‑inhibitor aliskiren. Both cilostazol and aliskiren share a contraindication in patients with فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), meaning that use of either drug should be avoided in individuals who have a known hypersensitivity reaction. The class Angiotensin‑Converting Enzyme Inhibitors [MoA] is characterized by the mechanism of action exemplified by rescinnamine, and this mechanism is contraindicated when combined with the mechanism of aliskiren. Consequently, co‑administration of aliskiren with ACE‑inhibitor therapy requires caution because the pharmacologic actions of aliskiren are opposed by the ACE‑inhibitor mechanism. Together, these relationships highlight important structural commonalities, shared hypersensitivity contraindications, and drug‑interaction considerations that are relevant for safe patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 692
agent2_snippets.content: 1H‑tetrazole, amidy and azoly are each described as inverse‑is‑a relationships to the antiplatelet drug cilostazol, indicating that they belong to distinct chemical or pharmacologic classes separate from cilostazol. Cilostazol is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Aliskiren, a direct renin inhibitor, is linked to azoly through an inverse‑is‑a relationship and is mapped from amidy, suggesting structural or functional similarity with these entities; aliskiren also carries a contraindication for hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Thus, both cilostazol and aliskiren share the same hypersensitivity precaution. The mechanism of action of the Angiotensin‑Converting Enzyme Inhibitors [MoA] class is associated with rescinnamine, and ACE inhibitors have a contraindicated mechanism of action when combined with aliskiren, reflecting the clinical recommendation to avoid concurrent use because of excessive renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system blockade. Methyldopa is classified as a centrally acting anti‑adrenergic agent, is a member of the methyldopa drug class, and is likewise contraindicated in individuals with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This underscores that hypersensitivity is a common precaution across diverse drug categories, including antiplatelet agents, direct renin inhibitors, ACE inhibitors (via their mechanism), and centrally acting antihypertensives.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
agent2_snippets.content: Agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system comprise several categories of angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). The “plain” ARBs—referred to as Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain or angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), plain—are a direct subclass of agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system. This plain ARB subclass includes the individual drugs candesartan, eprosartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, valsartan, and azilsartan medoxomil. In addition to the plain formulations, there are combination products that pair ARBs with other therapeutic agents; these combination ARBs are classified under “Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers,” indicating that ARB‑calcium‑channel‑blocker combinations form a distinct subgroup within the broader ARB category. Together, these hierarchical relationships clarify how individual ARB medications fit into the larger framework of renin‑angiotensin system–targeted therapies and how combination formulations are organized alongside plain agents.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 600
agent2_snippets.content: 1H‑tetrazole is a chemical scaffold that underlies several pharmacologic agents, including the antiplatelet drug cilostazol, the renin inhibitor aliskiren, and the compounds amidy and azoly, each of which is linked to cilostazol through an inverse‑is‑a relationship indicating shared structural features. Amidy and azoly are themselves structurally related to cilostazol, and aliskiren is further connected to azoly by an inverse‑is‑a link and is mapped from amidy, highlighting a derivation or close chemical similarity among these molecules. The mechanism‑of‑action class “Angiotensin‑Converting Enzyme Inhibitors [MoA]” is the mechanistic basis for the drug rescinnamine, indicating that rescinnamine exerts its therapeutic effect by inhibiting ACE. In contrast, aliskiren’s mechanism of action is listed as having a contraindicated mechanism with the ACE‑inhibitor class, suggesting that aliskiren should not be used in contexts where ACE inhibition is required or may cause adverse interactions. Both aliskiren and the beta‑blocker bisoprolol share a specific contraindication for the condition described as “انقطاع البول” (urinary retention), indicating that patients at risk for urinary retention should avoid these agents. Overall, the knowledge base connects a common 1H‑tetrazole core to multiple drugs, clarifies structural and derivation relationships among cilostazol, aliskiren, amidy, and azoly, delineates the ACE‑inhibitor mechanism of rescinnamine while flagging aliskiren’s incompatibility with that mechanism, and identifies urinary retention as a shared contraindication for aliskiren and bisoprolol.
